# Psalm 45: A Song of the King Greater Than All Kings

The psalmist begins with a trembling heart, overflowing with words about a noble theme. But notice what he's really doing—he's singing about a king who is addressed as "God" in verse 6. This is not merely a royal panegyric, a flattering song for a human monarch. The text moves deliberately from depicting an earthly king—mighty, victorious, just—to naming Him explicitly as God. His throne endures forever and ever. His justice is the scepter of His kingdom. He is anointed above all His companions with the oil of joy because He has loved righteousness and hated wickedness. This is either profound heresy or profound truth about a King who transcends human kingship entirely. The early church understood this psalm as speaking of Jesus Christ, the eternal Son of God, who alone sits on a throne that will never be shaken, who alone embodies perfect justice, and who alone deserves the worship of all nations.

What makes this psalm breathtaking is that it captures both Christ's majesty and His militant purpose. He is not a distant, ornamental king. He rides forth in victory on behalf of truth, humility, and justice. His arrows pierce the hearts of His enemies. The nations fall beneath His feet. This is the Christ who came not only to be gentle but to conquer, not only to suffer but to triumph. He does not compromise with wickedness. He does not negotiate with those who oppose His kingdom. Yet His warfare is waged for truth and justice—not for personal aggrandizement or vengeance, but for the establishment of what is actually right. This should reshape how we think about Christ's power and authority. His strength is not brutal; it is perfectly calibrated to His holy purposes.

The latter half of the psalm turns to the bride—the Daughter of Tyre, the princess adorned in gold and embroidered garments, brought into union with the king. The church has long understood this as picturing the bride of Christ, called to abandon her former allegiances and cleave wholly to the King. The call is direct and demanding: "Forget your people and your father's house...bow to him, for he is your lord." This is not romantic sentiment but radical realignment. To follow Christ is not to add Him to what we already possess; it is to relinquish competing loyalties and recognize His absolute lordship. His sons will become princes, His name will be commemorated through all generations, and the nations will praise Him forever. But this inheritance belongs only to those who truly make Him king—not merely in word, but in the displacement of all other authorities and affections from the throne of the heart.
